wb_txt_shrt: Bronze inscription panel with relief letters in the ancient Yemeni alphabet (Old South Arabian), probably in the language of the Kingdom of Qataban. wb_txt_lg: Bronze inscription panel with relief letters in the ancient Yemeni alphabet, also known as Old South Arabian, probably in the language of the Kingdom of Qataban. The panel is rectangular and bears eight lines of evenly spaced horizontal characters, with a narrower top line containing only a few characters in a smaller font size. The object is described as a rare bronze inscription panel and is associated with funerary representation in ancient Saudi Arabia, where anthropomorphic funerary stelae with relief heads, headdresses, and funerary inscriptions were sometimes carved in stone or cast in bronze. Dimensions are 4 in W x 5.65 in H (10.2 cm x 14.4 cm).

wb_txt_shrt: Head, alabaster, hand-carved in high relief, perhaps from a funerary stele. The male face has almond-shaped eyes, thin brows, a slender nose, pursed lips, protruding rectangular ears, and a stocky neck. Size: 5.6 in. W x 9.375 in. H (14.2 cm x 23.8 cm).
wb_txt_lg: Head, alabaster, hand-carved in high relief, perhaps from a funerary stele. The object presents a male visage with gazing almond-shaped eyes beneath thin brows, a slender nose above pursed lips, protruding rectangular ears, and a stocky neck. The surface is described as quixotic in expression, and the head is noted as stylistically similar to another example with comparable facial hair stippling. The text identifies this type of figural representation as belonging to a tradition of alabaster and other stone stele with high relief faces honoring the deceased. Size: 5.6 in. W x 9.375 in. H (14.2 cm x 23.8 cm).
looted_text: Lot, art, dimensions 14.2 × 23.8.

wb_txt_shrt: Four cast bronze bull hooves, each with a cuboid upper element and two naturalistic projections at the back, probably intended as supports for a table, altar, or naos. Each measures 5.75 in. W x 11 in. H (14.6 cm x 27.9 cm) and is dated to the 1st millennium BCE to the early 1st millennium CE.
wb_txt_lg: Four cast bronze bull hooves, each more-than-life-sized and similarly proportioned, with a realistic hoof form showing two claws, a cuboid element set slightly over the back of the hoof, and two naturalistic projections from the rear. Two of the examples have projecting slots on top of the cuboid, indicating attachment points. The form suggests that they may have served as supports for a table or altar rather than for a full bull statue; another possibility is that they belonged to a naos, a simple rectangular temple form. Each measures 5.75 in. W x 11 in. H (14.6 cm x 27.9 cm). The group is dated broadly to the 1st millennium BCE to the early 1st millennium CE, probably later in that range based on the lead weight mentioned in the source text.
looted_text: Hooves, bronze. A group of identical oversized bull hooves in bronze, each larger than normal size and larger than a large cube. Two examples have prominent holes above the cube for installation, and three examples are hollow inside, with holes at the back of the cubes and on the foot base.

wb_txt_shrt: Figure, alabaster. South Arabian seated figure of the Sun Goddess with bulbous coiffure, long nose, incised arched brows, and large inlaid white stone pupils remaining; the arms are bent at the elbows and held out in front, with one hand clenched and pierced vertically for an attachment and the other held open. wb_txt_lg: Figure, alabaster. South Arabian seated figure of the Sun Goddess, with a bulbous coiffure, long nose, incised arched brows, and large inlaid white stone pupils remaining. The arms are bent at the elbows and held out in front; one hand is clenched and pierced vertically for an attachment, while the other is held open. The figure stands on a square pedestal with a finely incised three-line Old South Arabian dedicatory inscription reading: “Am’al Kalan Barntum, and Haywu and Safliyan have dedicated (this monument) to Dha t’Adihanum, as all his dedicator’s due”. The inscription is described as a dedication to the goddess, to settle the dedicators’ account with the deity. Height: 35.50 cm. Reference 21362.

wb_txt_shrt: Figurine, alabaster: a realistically carved South Arabian camel, depicted as a dromedary standing four-square with head erect.
wb_txt_lg: Figurine, alabaster: a realistically carved South Arabian camel, identified as a dromedary, standing four-square with its head erect. The object is described as belonging to Ancient South Arabia, a geographical label for a region occupied by the kingdoms of Sabaʼ, Qatabān, Ma‘īn, Ḥaḍramawt, the Kingdom of Awsan, and the Himyarite Kingdom. The source notes that the territory of these kingdoms corresponds to parts of modern-day Yemen and Oman, extending north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and as far as the East African coast, including modern Tanzania. The text also states that animal depictions in this region had significance in religious rituals and as mythical creatures, as well as as incarnations or symbols of gods and goddesses.
looted_text: Camel statue, marble, standing on a rectangular base, with a pointed sleeve, a neck curved upward to the highest point, and the head emphasized to the front; dated to the 1st–2nd century BCE.

wb_txt_lg: Statuette, bronze: South Arabian hollow-cast bronze statuette of a male rider, rendered in a stylised form. A triangular opening is present on both the chest and back, and a belt is wrapped around the waist, with short vertical grooves indicating clothing on the straight legs. The figure was likely mounted on an animal, possibly a camel, also modelled in bronze. The object measures 5 cm in height, excluding the stand. South Arabia is identified in the source as a region corresponding to parts of modern-day Yemen and extending into Oman, north to the Arabian oasis of Dedan, to Ethiopia, and as far as the East African coast into modern Tanzania.

The broad central band is composed of lobed rectangular panels containing scrolling arabesques around quatrefoil roundels, with panel borders that entwine and continue into lobed concentric quatrefoils dividing each panel. Below is a band of stylized thuluth calligraphy interrupted by roundels containing quatrefoil panels. The base and interior are plain, with remains of monochrome lacquer. wb_txt_shrt: Coin, gold. Rasulid gold dinar of al-Mu’ayyad Da’ud (696–721 AH), struck at ‘Adan in 718 AH.
wb_txt_lg: Coin, gold. Rasulid gold dinar of al-Mu’ayyad Da’ud (696–721 AH), struck at ‘Adan in 718 AH. The source notes that, unlike the latter, the Rasulids included both the mint name and date in their legends. These gold issues may have been issued to facilitate trade with India.
looted_text: Coin, gold, a Muslim Yemeni two-faced coin dated 718 H, weighing 4.34 g. One face is surrounded by a four-tier Arabic inscription band and has a square frame containing an Arabic inscription reading “There is no god but God, Muhammad is the messenger of God”; the other face bears an Arabic inscription reading “Sultan King in Support [...] David.” The object is described as in excellent condition.

wb_txt_shrt: Plaque, bronze. South Arabian bronze votive plaque, framed with a raised flat border and inscribed with seven lines of Qatabanian script mentioning a dedication of bronze. Circa 1st-2nd century B.C.; 14¼ in x 11 in (36 cm x 28 cm).
wb_txt_lg: Plaque, bronze. South Arabian bronze votive plaque, framed with a raised flat border and inscribed with seven lines of Qatabanian script. The inscription mentions a dedication of bronze and refers to the plaque itself. The object is dated to circa 1st-2nd century B.C. Dimensions are 14¼ in x 11 in (36 cm x 28 cm).
looted_text: Plaque, bronze, engraved with seven lines of text on the surface; rectangular form, measuring 14 × 11 in. (36 × 28 cm); dated to the 1st–2nd century BC.

wb_txt_shrt: Furniture, paintings, coins, currency, stamps, home décor, fashion, Chinese antiques, photography, watches, rings, oil paintings, toys, dolls, games, clocks, Japanese antiques, rugs and carpets, prints and multiples, and other auction categories are listed, along with multiple upcoming and live sales.
wb_txt_lg: Furniture, paintings, coins, currency, stamps, home décor, fashion, Chinese antiques, photography, watches, rings, oil paintings, toys, dolls, games, clocks, Japanese antiques, rugs and carpets, prints and multiples, and related categories are listed in an auction marketplace overview. The text also includes references to spring finds, early 20th century design, California and Western art, fine jewelry, European furniture, trending items, and multiple auction listings with dates, locations, and sale titles. No single object is described in detail beyond these category and auction references.
looted_text: Head, marble, of a statue of a man, carved in a clear-cut style with two eyes, two eyebrows, and a mouth; dated to the 1st century B.C./1st century A.D. according to the source. The object is described as in good condition. Source attribution in the text mentions Hansokrams (1912–1992), Düsseldorf, Germany.

wb_txt_shrt: Auction platform, not an archaeological object.
wb_txt_lg: Auction platform, not an archaeological object. The source text refers to "BidsSpirit Israel - Auctions: jewelry, art, antiquities and more," which is commercial and does not provide a describable object, material, typology, dimensions, or period.
looted_text: Statue, bronze, in the form of a camel. The figure is described as surrounding the top of the neck of a rope, with a tranquil inscription along the blade line that is unclear, and the body covered with a full layer of effort. Height 7.5 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Jewelry, art, and antiquities auction listing.
wb_txt_lg: Auction listing for jewelry, art, antiquities, and other objects. No specific object, material, typology, dimensions, or period is provided in the source text.
looted_text: Statue, bronze, depicting a standing figure with one arm extended forward and the other extended to the side, mounted on a base; the body is covered by a rust layer that obscures details, including an unclear graft line. Dated to 4.2 BC. Height 11.5 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette votive en bronze représentant un dignitaire debout, vêtu d'un long pagne et tenant une longue canne de la main gauche. La coiffure est formée de courtes mèches. H. 13,5 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette votive en bronze représentant un dignitaire debout, de type sud-arabique, vêtu d'un long pagne et tenant une longue canne de la main gauche. La coiffure est formée de courtes mèches. L'objet mesure 13,5 cm de hauteur.
looted_text: Statue, bronze, of a standing man in motion, with a clear face, muzzle, hair, wrinkle, and sailor attributes as described in the source text. The object is recorded as missing. It is dated to the second century BCE and attributed to the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Dimensions: 13.5 cm long.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, alabaster. Fragment of a South Arabian sculpted stela with a stylized frontal face, a long straight nose, and hollow eyes originally inlaid. A fragment of a South Arabian inscription is preserved at the base.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, alabaster. Fragment of a sculpted stela depicting a stylized frontal face with a long straight nose and hollow eyes originally intended for inlay. The base preserves part of a South Arabian inscription, identified in the source as the owner's name. South Arabian Peninsula, late 1st millennium BC. Height: 27.5 cm.
looted_text: Tombstone, marble, fragmentary, with incised inscription and relief decoration; described as a man’s tombstone, preserving part of the deceased’s name with the letter “N” visible in the final line, and featuring a human face motif with prominent eyes, a small mouth, and a strand or line beneath the mouth. Provenance is given as the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Length 27.5 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Iconic head, material not specified. Small sculpted head representing a man with short hair, a triangular face, a long narrow nose, and a short mouth; a lightly raised beard collar extends up to the ears. Height: 12.5 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Iconic head, material not specified. This small-scale head was generally mounted on inscribed limestone bases bearing the owner's name. The present example represents a man with short hair exposing the ears. The face is triangular in shape, with a long, slender nose above a short mouth, and a beard collar in slight relief rising to the ears. Height: 12.5 cm.
looted_text: Statue head, marble: head of a male statue with a long neck, carved in marble; the face has high eyebrows, prominent eyes, a prominent nose, and a small mouth, with hair rendered in a clear, salty tombstone-like treatment.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, South Arabian alabaster and limestone, H. 22 cm. Iconic stela representing a face shown frontally, with a triangular nose above a narrow mouth.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, South Arabian alabaster and limestone, height 22 cm. Iconic stela depicting a face in frontal view. The facial features are rendered schematically, with a triangular nose surmounting a narrow mouth. The source text identifies the object as a South Arabian stela made of alabaster and limestone and gives its height as 22 cm.
looted_text: Grave marker head, polished marble, in the form of a man’s head with a short neck, defined head, indicated hair, eyebrows, a prominent nose, and closed mouth, decorated with two lizards and two rosettes; from the Horn of the Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen); length 22 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Figurine, bronze, depicting two lizards side by side in aerial view. South Arabian Peninsula, 1st millennium BC. Length 6.2 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Figurine, bronze, depicting two lizards side by side in aerial view. The object is described as South Arabian and dated to the 1st millennium BC. It measures 6.2 cm in length. The source text identifies the subject as two lizards shown côte à côte, with the figure presented in plan view.
looted_text: Statue, bronze, depicting two adjoining lizards connected at the tail and shown in motion. The object is almost 10 cm in diameter.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, South Arabian alabaster, H. 28 cm, L. 23.2 cm. Historiated funerary stela depicting a man and a woman, with a three-line inscription at the top.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, South Arabian alabaster, H. 28 cm, L. 23.2 cm. Historiated funerary stela depicting a man and a woman. The upper part bears a three-line inscription, identified in the source as beginning with the words "Stèle sépulcrale et monument de Ratum". The object is described as South Arabian and made of alabaster.
looted_text: Palette, stone, with incised and carved decoration, 28 cm high and 23 cm wide, from the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen), dated to the 2nd century AD. The object is described as a palette with drawings, showing sculptural decoration and a three-line inscription on the anthem line reading NFS, NBYR, BAT, BA, B.

wb_txt_shrt: Head of a statue, alabaster, South Arabian, H. 14 cm. Male head with the bridge of the nose continuing into the eyebrows; the pupils are engraved. The beard and moustache are indicated by stippling, and the coiffure, probably in stucco, was added separately.
wb_txt_lg: Head of a statue, alabaster, South Arabian, height 14 cm. The object is a male head from a statue, with the bridge of the nose continuing into the eyebrows and the pupils of the eyes engraved. The beard and moustache are rendered by stippling. The coiffure, probably made of stucco, was applied separately. The description identifies the piece as a South Arabian alabaster head of a man.
looted_text: Statue head, stone, of a male figure with a clearly modeled face and distinct eyes; carved in stone, from the southern Arabian Peninsula (ancient Yemen), dated to the 3rd century B.C.E., and measuring almost 20 cm in height.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, alabaster. South Arabian aniconic stela of slightly trapezoidal form, rounded at the top, with a rectangular plinth engraved with the owner's name. H. 41.5 cm; W. 24 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, alabaster. South Arabian aniconic stela of slightly trapezoidal form, rounded at the top, with a rectangular plinth engraved with the owner's name. The object measures 41.5 cm in height and 24 cm in width. According to the source text, such stelae were placed in a niche or against a wall.
looted_text: Plaque, marble. Rectangular polished marble plaque with a base of the same stone, bearing five letters: “A click and HBL” and the inscription “Horn A and Miledi - Southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen).” Height 36 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Figure iconique, material not specified, cylindrical form with a sculpted stylized face at the top and a mouth marked by an incision.
wb_txt_lg: Figure iconique, material not specified, of cylindrical form, with the upper part sculpted as a stylized face. The mouth is indicated by an incision. The source text notes that such figurines were generally fixed onto inscribed bases.
looted_text: Tombstone, marble, cylindrical in shape, with a clear-cut surface, two perforations, and a small incised mouth; described as having a distinctive character in the making of gravestones. Origin: southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Height 18 cm. Missing.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette, limestone, depicting an ibex on a quadrangular base, with very developed horns. South Arabian.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette, limestone, depicting an ibex standing on a quadrangular base, with very developed horns. The object is described as South Arabian and measures H. 32.5 cm. A comparative bibliography is cited: Queen of Sheba, exhibition catalogue, London, 2002.
looted_text: Animal statue, stone. Statue of an animal on a rectangular base of the same material, with large horns visible; described as a “glimpse of big horns wrapped up.” Stone, 32.5 cm high. Dated to the 5th century B.C.E. and attributed to the southern Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen).

wb_txt_shrt: Oil lamp, bronze, with edges decorated with ivy scrolls and the rear bearing a South Arabian inscription of three letters.
wb_txt_lg: Oil lamp, bronze, with a large form and edges ornamented with ivy scrolls. The rear is decorated with a South Arabian inscription consisting of three letters. The object is described as a large oil lamp; no further technical details, dimensions, or chronology are provided in the source text.
looted_text: Object, bronze. Curved handle ending in an animal body with large horns curved backwards; two hands placed in front of a lumbar area bearing a three-letter inscription. Sacrificed. Dated to the 4th century B.C.E.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, South Arabian limestone, H. 45.5 cm, L. 18.2 cm. Rectangular stela with a stylised triangular face at the top and an inscription of three South Arabian letters identifying the owner. South Arabian Peninsula, late 1st millennium BC.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, South Arabian limestone, H. 45.5 cm, L. 18.2 cm. Rectangular stela carved with a stylised triangular face at the top. It is engraved with an inscription in three South Arabian letters, identified in the source as the name of the owner. The object is described as an iconic stela and is attributed to the South Arabian Peninsula, late 1st millennium BC.
looted_text: Tombstone, stone. Rectangular tombstone carved in low relief with a clear-cut discharge, featuring eyes, a prominent nose, and a semi-open mouth; inscribed with three letters reading p.P.R. Late 2000 BCE. Length 45.5 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, alabaster. South Arabian rectangular icon stela with a stylised frontal face carved at the top. The face has a long, thin, triangular nose flanked by engraved eyes with indicated pupils.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, alabaster. South Arabian rectangular icon stela, sculpted at the top with a stylised frontal face. The facial features are rendered in a simplified manner, with a long, thin, triangular nose flanked by engraved eyes and indicated pupils. The object is described as a rectangular stela carved at the summit with a stylised face seen from the front. Comparative bibliography cited in the source: Yémen, au pays de la reine de Saba, exhibition catalogue, Paris, 1997.
looted_text: Tombstone, marble. Rectangular tombstone carved from a marble stone, from the Horn of the Arabian Peninsula (old Yemen). Length 38.2 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Sword (saif) and scabbard, silver-plated steel and silver-plated metal, Yemen, 19th century. Single-edged triple-fullered curved steel blade with a silver-plated hilt and engraved decoration, accompanied by a silver-plated scabbard with engraved vegetal motifs.
wb_txt_lg: Sword (saif) and scabbard, silver-plated steel and silver-plated metal, Yemen, 19th century. The sword measures 80 cm and has a single-edged, triple-fullered curved steel blade set with a silver-plated hilt decorated with engraved floral scrolls. The silver-plated scabbard is decorated with engraved vegetal motifs. The object is described as being in overall good condition, with minor patination to the blade, minor oxidation, minor dents, and the knuckle chain missing.
looted_text: Sword, polished metal and silver, with a blade of polished metal and a silver hilt or head decorated with silver filaments.

wb_txt_shrt: Brazier, brass, second half 13th century. Probably made in Egypt. The object is associated with Rasulid Sultan al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar and bears a monumental inscription naming the patron.
wb_txt_lg: Brazier, brass, second half 13th century. Probably made in Egypt. This portable brazier was used as a grill and heater. It has lion-headed knobs with rings that served as receptacles for handles to transport the heated unit, and confronted dragon heads on each side that functioned as spit brackets. The monumental inscription names and honors the patron, identified as the second ruler of the Rasulid dynasty, al-Malik al-Muzaffar Shams al-Din Yusuf ibn 'Umar (r. 1250–95). His dynastic emblem, a five-petalled rosette on a circular shield, appears prominently on both sides of each corner bracket.
looted_text: Metal artifact, metal, rectangular conglomerate on four legs, with edges decorated with geometric shapes; the sides are covered with a walrus-enveloped decorator and a restaurant with prominent animal head feces, and the inter-lettered emptiness includes various plant decorative elements.

wb_txt_shrt: Double statuette, material not specified.
wb_txt_lg: Double statuette, material not specified. The catalogue entry identifies the object as a double statuette and provides no further technical description, dimensions, chronology, or provenance in the source text. The lot was offered in an archaeological sale and is recorded with an estimate of 3000–3500 EUR and a result of 5,800 EUR.
looted_text: Human statue, limestone, depicting two interconnected human figures in a similar salt-like material. The figures have prominent eyes and noses, heads joined by a small cylindrical bridge, and short hands held against the chest.

wb_txt_shrt: No object description is provided in the source text; it contains only auction notices and general statements about condition reports and dimensions.
wb_txt_lg: No archaeological object is described in the source text. The text consists of auction information and general notices concerning the condition of lots, possible damages or restorations, and the non-guaranteed nature of dimensions given in catalogue descriptions or condition reports. No object type, material, technique, typology, dimensions, or period is specified.
looted_text: Human statue, sandstone, a woman’s bust with a visible head, large eyes inlaid with gypsum and cream stones, high eyebrows with stone inserts, a high sharp nose, full lips, hair arranged in lines and falling behind the neck, and two distinct ears.

wb_txt_shrt: Figure of a woman, alabaster, Qataban, 3rd century B.C./1st century A.D. South Arabian sculpture carved from translucent calcite-alabaster, with a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and recessed eyes.
wb_txt_lg: Figure of a woman, alabaster, Qataban, 3rd century B.C./1st century A.D. South Arabian sculpture carved from translucent calcite-alabaster. The figure is described as displaying the stylized aesthetic characteristic of South Arabian figurative art, with a frontal pose, simplified bodily forms, and an emphasis on facial presence and symmetry. The recessed eyes are noted as extant, and the text states that such figures may originally have featured painted details or inlaid facial detailing. The object is identified as a South Arabian alabaster figure of a woman from Qataban.
looted_text: Statue, marble, human figure represented as a woman’s bust with jealous eyes retaining traces of black fixing material, a straight nose, a small mouth, and curly hair falling behind the shoulders.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette of woman, H. 19 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette of woman, height 19 cm. The source text provides no further information on material, technique, typology, date, or condition beyond the object designation and stated height.
looted_text: Human statue, material not specified. A human statue described in the source as “A human statue.”

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette, material not specified, representing a woman, possibly pregnant, wearing a long tunic and extending her arms forward. Her hairstyle is encircled by a diadem and she wears a necklace with a pendant. Height: 13.2 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette, material not specified, representing a woman, possibly pregnant, wearing a long tunic and extending her arms forward. The hairstyle is encircled by a diadem, and the figure wears a necklace with a pendant. The object is described as a statuette and measures 13.2 cm in height.
looted_text: Human statue, bronze, depicting a standing woman with a pronounced pregnant abdomen. The head is upright, with a gang and a clearly defined ellipse wrapped backwards and hanging behind the shoulders. The arms are extended outward and the hands are open. The garment covers the body to mid-thigh. The legs are clearly modelled, with distinct toes. Height: 13.2 cm. Date: toward the 2nd century BC.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette votive plate in an unspecified material, representing a stylised man.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette votive plate in an unspecified material, representing a stylised man. The object is described as a votive plate statuette and belongs to the field of archaeology. No further technical details, dimensions, or dating are provided in the source text.
looted_text: Human statue, bronze, small flat-shaped figure representing a man in an abstract manner, standing in a long dress with both arms extended forward and holding a stick in the right hand; the figure is connected below to a small stabilization base. First millennium BC. Height: 13.7 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Four votive statuettes, material not specified, representing stylised figures, one seated and headless. South Arabian Peninsula, 1st millennium BC. Height: 2.2 to 4.2 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Four votive statuettes, material not specified, representing stylised human figures, including one seated acéphale. The group is attributed to the South Arabian Peninsula and dated to the 1st millennium BC. The recorded height ranges from 2.2 cm to 4.2 cm. No further technical or contextual details are provided in the source text.
looted_text: Statuettes, bronze, four votive figurines representing human figures in an abstract manner, with unclear features; three are standing with outstretched, separate arms, and one is seated with the head missing; all are covered with a green layer of bronze corrosion. First millennium BC. Height: 2.2 to 4.2 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Fragment of a large statue, bronze, representing the torso of a cuirassed man. H. 21 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Fragment of a large statue, bronze, representing the torso of a cuirassed man. The object preserves the upper part of the body and is described as a fragment of a large-scale statue. Height: 21 cm.
looted_text: Human statue fragment, bronze, preserving part of a hollow bronze figure: the left torso and shoulder, with a shield and a belt crossing the shoulders and extending to the back. First and second century C.E. Height: 21 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Sculptural fragment, stone, main right hand from a life-size statue, with the fingers slightly bent. Height 13 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Sculptural fragment, stone, main right hand from a life-size statue, with the fingers slightly bent. The object is described as a right hand originating from a life-size statue, preserving the fingers in a slightly flexed position. The recorded height is 13 cm.
looted_text: Statue, bronze: human statue preserving the bronze palm of the right hand, rendered at natural size with long fingers and covered with a green layer of bronze corrosion. Length: 13 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Stèle iconique en pierre de type Myriam, with ears treated geometrically, incised eyebrows, and oval eyes inlaid with calcite.
wb_txt_lg: Stèle iconique en pierre de type Myriam. The ears are applied and treated in a geometric manner, the eyebrows are incised, and the eyes are oval and inlaid with calcite. The source text provides no further information on dimensions, date, provenance, or technique beyond the described carved and inlaid features.
looted_text: Statue, material not specified. Human statue depicting a woman, with a clearly rendered head and well-executed features. The eyes are oval, the eyebrows are arched and raised, the nose is prominent and sharp with a rounded end, and the mouth is represented by a curved line.

wb_txt_shrt: Statue head, material not specified, H. 17 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statue head, material not specified, height 17 cm. The catalogue entry provides no further technical or typological details beyond the object designation and measurement.
looted_text: Statue head, marble, human figure, carved in stone. The object is described as the head of a statue, with a human form, made of marble.

wb_txt_shrt: Portrait of a dignitary, height 23 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Portrait of a dignitary, height 23 cm. The source text identifies the object as a rare portrait of a dignitary and gives its height as 23 cm. No further information is provided in the source text regarding material, technique, date, provenance, or condition.
looted_text: Human statue, marble. Statue of a man carved in white marble, with a clearly shaped and sharply cut head. The face is described as gloomy, with semi-oval carved eyes, eyebrows above the eyes, a pierced nose, full and relaxed lips, and ears clearly indicated on both sides of the head and pierced. The hairstyle consists of short locks arranged in square and rectangular forms, and the neck is broad.

wb_txt_shrt: Auction notice, text on paper, concerning condition, dimensions, bidding, VAT, and pre-emption rights.
wb_txt_lg: Auction notice, text on paper, giving general sale conditions and catalogue disclaimers. It states that, because of age and nature, many lots are not in their original condition and that catalogue or condition report descriptions may mention damage and/or restoration for inspection purposes only. It also notes that dimensions are provided for information only and are not guaranteed. The text includes a notice that for lots sold by a non-resident seller, the buyer must pay 5.5% VAT in addition to the hammer price, refundable upon proof of export outside the EEC within one month. It further states that if two simultaneous bids are recognized, the lot will be offered again, and that a representative of the French State may exercise the pre-emption right during the auction, with the declaration recorded in the official sale record.
looted_text: Human statue, bronze. Head of a bronze statue representing a young man with a clear gaze; the eyes are prominent, the nose is straight, and the mouth has full lips. The hair is arranged in strands ending in forward and side waves, and the head is crowned with a vegetal wreath surmounted by a decorative element.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette, material not specified, of a ram. L. 13.5 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette, material not specified, in the form of a ram. The object is recorded with a length of 13.5 cm. No further technical, chronological, or condition information is provided in the source text.
looted_text: Animal statue, marble, depicting a standing sheep with a clearly stylized form. The animal has large curved horns with spiral or snail-like terminals, a marked head, eyes not clearly represented, and a mouth indicated by a curved line. The body is cylindrical, and the statue stands on four legs. Dated to the second half of the 1st millennium BCE.

wb_txt_shrt: Statuette, material not specified, representing a young leaping cervid with the body slightly turned to the right. Height: 25.5 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Statuette, material not specified, representing a young leaping cervid with the body slightly turned to the right. The figure is described as a young cervid in motion, with a dynamic pose and a slight turn of the body toward the right. Height: 25.5 cm.
looted_text: Animal statue, bronze, depicting a young male deer in a crouching position with the body turned to the right. The head is defined, with prominent ears and short horns. The body is rendered in a fluid manner, and the front legs are missing. The surface is covered with a layer of green bronze corrosion. Dated to the second half of the first millennium BC.

wb_txt_shrt: Stone pillar stela, Ancient South Arabian, dated to the 1st century B.C.
wb_txt_lg: Stone pillar stela, made of stone and attributed to Ancient South Arabian culture, dated to the 1st century B.C. The object is identified as a pillar stela; no further technical, dimensional, or inscriptional details are provided in the source text.
looted_text: Tombstone, limestone. Rectangular grave marker with a human face in relief or carved depiction, showing raised eyebrows, rectangular nose, and a white mouth; described as “Jerry’s Stone.” Dated to the first century BCE.

wb_txt_shrt: No object is described in the source text; it contains only auction and condition-report notices relating to archaeological lots.
wb_txt_lg: No object type, material, typology, dimensions, period, or technical description is provided in the source text. The text consists of general auction information and disclaimers concerning the condition of lots, possible damages or restorations, and the non-guaranteed nature of dimensions stated in the catalogue or condition report. It does not contain a specific archaeological object record.
looted_text: Tombstone, limestone. Irregular grave marker carved in limestone, with a sculpted human face. The source text describes it as an irregular, semi-rivaled grave of limestone and notes that the face was sculpted on a human face; no further details are provided.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, stone, inscribed with the name of the deceased. This large inscribed stela was probably set into the ground at the place where the body lay.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, stone, large inscribed monument bearing the name of the deceased. The object is described as probably having been set into the ground at the very spot where the body rested. No further technical details, dimensions, or dating are provided in the source text.
looted_text: Object, material not specified, with dimensions 3.5 x 25.5.

wb_txt_shrt: Stela, stone, representing the head of a man with a triangular face and barely indicated hair. A basin with a crenellated rim is present at the base of the neck. South Arabian Peninsula, 1st millennium BC. Height: 67 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Stela, stone, in the form of a large iconic representation of a man's head, with a triangular face and hair only lightly indicated. An inscription is probably apocryphal. The object has a basin with a crenellated rim at the base of the neck. It is noted as being in very good condition, with a few chips. South Arabian Peninsula, 1st millennium BC. Height: 67 cm.
looted_text: Object type not specified, material not specified. The source text only gives the name “PERRE BERGE & Associes” and does not provide any archaeological or object-specific information.

wb_txt_shrt: Votive plaque, stone, inscribed with eight lines in Qatabanite characters. It preserves a dedication by Sa'adil of the clan of Ab'am to Hawkam Nabat. Height 31.5 cm; width 26.6 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Votive plaque, stone, inscribed with eight lines in Qatabanite characters. The inscription records a dedication by Sa'adil of the clan of Ab'am to Hawkam Nabat. The proper names in this dedication are already known from other Qatabanite inscriptions. Palaeographically, the monument appears to be close to periods C and D of A. Petite lacune at the lower left corner; otherwise in very good condition. The deity Hawkam is identified in the source as a goddess. Height 31.5 cm; width 26.6 cm.
looted_text: Tablet, bronze, with an inscription and window holes distributed on three angles for stabilization. The engraved text records a vow made by a person named Saad El for the Abed, who was tried at Shebhan’s temple, invoking the gods Athtar, uncle, and prophet; the vow was made on his behalf and on behalf of his brother and his son. Dimensions recorded as 31.5 x 26.6 cm.

wb_txt_shrt: Funerary stele, carved stone, rectangular stele sculpted in relief with the frontal busts of three stylized figures surmounted by a South Arabian inscription. H. 26 cm; L. 30 cm.
wb_txt_lg: Funerary stele, carved stone, rectangular in form and sculpted with the frontal busts of three stylized figures, surmounted by a South Arabian inscription. The object measures 26 cm in height and 30 cm in length. The source text identifies it as a funerary stele and provides no further technical details beyond its carved execution, rectangular shape, figural relief decoration, and inscription.
looted_text: Object type and material cannot be determined from the provided text, which appears to contain only a partial inscription or catalog notation with no descriptive information about the object itself.

wb_txt_shrt: Auction notice for an archaeology sale, with information on VAT, bidding, public viewing, experts, and sale conditions.
wb_txt_lg: Auction notice, printed text on paper, relating to an archaeology sale held at Drouot Richelieu in salles 8 and 9, with lots 1 to 104 sold at 11:00 and lots 105 to 482 sold at 14:00. The text gives public viewing times for Friday 16 January 2009 and Saturday 17 January 2009, names the archaeological expert Christophe Kunicki, and sets out bidding, pre-emption, payment, delivery, and guarantee conditions. wb_txt_shrt: Coin, silver. Ancient Greek posthumous tetradrachm of Alexander the Great, featuring the name and types of Alexander. The obverse shows Alexander wearing the Nemean lion-skin headdress associated with Herakles; the reverse depicts Zeus seated left on a throne, holding an eagle and sceptre, with the inscription ΑΛΕΞΑΝΔΡΟΥ.
wb_txt_lg: Coin, silver. Ancient Greek posthumous tetradrachm of Alexander the Great, attributed to the Hellenistic period and associated with the Near East (Western Asiatic) and Southern Europe. The tetradrachm was Alexander’s silver stater, or standard denomination, and was minted in greater numbers than any other Alexander denomination. The obverse depicts Alexander wearing the Nemean lion-skin headdress, one of Herakles’ typical attributes, and the reverse shows the Greek god Zeus seated to the left on a throne, holding an eagle in his right hand and a sceptre in his left. The Greek inscription ΑΛΕΞΑΝ∆ΡΟΥ is written vertically to the right of the figure of Zeus. The text describes the coin as a very rare, posthumous Alexander the Great silver tetradrachm featuring the name and types of Alexander.

wb_txt_lg: Candlestick, gilt-copper (tombak), Ottoman, Turkey, dated Dhu'l-Hijja 945 AH/May 1539 AD. The object has a large truncated conical body surmounted by a flat drip tray, a tall ridged cylindrical neck, and a tapered ridged candle holder. The base of the body is engraved with a thuluth inscription recording the endowment of the candlestick by Usta Ibrahim, described as the head cauldron maker and servant of Sultan Suleyman Khan, to the "tomb of His Holiness the Sultan of Prophets." The inscription gives the date as the middle of Dhu'l-Hijja 945 AH, corresponding to May 1539 AD. The text identifies the object as an Ottoman courtly metalwork piece associated with Suleyman the Magnificent.

wb_txt_shrt: Placeholder solution using Anubis proof-of-work to protect a website from aggressive scraping and to reduce the need to present challenge pages to likely legitimate users.
wb_txt_lg: Placeholder solution using Anubis proof-of-work to protect a server against aggressive scraping by AI companies. The system is described as a temporary measure intended to allow more time for fingerprinting and identifying headless browsers, for example through font rendering behavior, so that challenge proof-of-work pages do not need to be shown to users more likely to be legitimate. It uses a Proof-of-Work scheme in the vein of Hashcash, a proposed proof-of-work method for reducing email spam. The text notes that Anubis requires modern JavaScript features and that plugins such as JShelter may disable them, which can cause downtime and make website resources inaccessible to all users.

The decoration is divided by a blade-like line and includes a central inscription-like element, surrounded by vegetal ornament with grape clusters, birds feeding on grapes, plant forms, and a clearly rendered bull head with small birds also shown eating grapes.

Anubis uses a proof-of-work scheme in the vein of Hashcash, a proposed proof-of-work method for reducing email spam. wb_txt_shrt: Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. Funerary or votive object carved in a fully frontal pose, with a long plain garment and a base inscribed with the name Sadiqim Ma'ad.
wb_txt_lg: Standing male figure, alabaster (calcite), ca. late 1st millennium BCE. This funerary or votive object was intended for placement in a tomb or temple. The figure stands frontally and wears a long, plain garment reaching almost to the ankles. It is carved in one piece with a base that bears the name of the individual and his clan, Sadiqim Ma'ad. The eyes and eyebrows are carved to receive inlays, now missing. The arms are bent at the elbows and extend outward at right angles to the body; one hand is clenched in a fist and pierced vertically to hold an object, perhaps a scepter, now lost, while the other hand is open. The undersurface of the base and the top and back of the head are roughly carved, consistent with statues that were set in niches and left unfinished on non-visible surfaces. The ears are placed high on the head, and the aquiline nose, small mouth, and fully frontal pose are characteristic of human images made in southwestern Arabia.

wb_txt_shrt: Manuscript on paper, Menorat HaMaor by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav, written in Sana'a, Yemen, 1848. wb_txt_lg: Manuscript on paper, Menorat HaMaor by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav, written in Sana'a, Yemen, 1848. The manuscript is described as complete, apart from a number of index leaves missing at the end. At the beginning of each Ner, or chapter, there is red and black ornamentation, with the exception of the sixth and seventh Ners. The title page is illustrated with a two-colour frame and states that the book Menorat HaMaor was written by Rabbi Yitzchak Abuhav the Sephardi and written here in Sana'a in 1848. The text notes that Menorat HaMaor was widely embraced by Yemenite Jews and read in synagogues on Shabbat afternoon.
looted_text: Hebrew manuscript on paper, dated 1848, comprising 227 leaves and written in two columns per page with red ink headings and dialects. The manuscript includes red and black decorative elements at the beginning of each Nir, except Nirs VI and VII, and a framed title page (blonin) bearing the inscription that the book was written in Sana'a in 1848. Copies of the writer appear at the end of the book. It is described as a complete manuscript of formation, lacking a number of index leaves, and predating the well-known Livorno edition. Condition is noted as good.

wb_txt_shrt: Manuscript, paper, Mipi Chachamim, containing halachic, aggadic, and kabbalistic explanations of various laws and customs, Yemen, 1895.
wb_txt_lg: Manuscript, paper, Mipi Chachamim, with halachic, aggadic, and kabbalistic explanations for various laws and customs. The manuscript is described as having many variations from the printed version and as corresponding to another manuscript written nine months earlier by the same scribe, Rabbi Oded son of Yafet, in Iyar 1894, by the desire of his master, Zecharya. It includes a long letter addressed to Rabbi Salam son of Menachem by Yuda Nissim, Chassan Yuda, and Machbuv Avni, concerning aliyah to Eretz Israel and their travails, and contains a colophon by the scribe Rabbi Oded son of Yafet, identified as Ra'avad of Aden, Ibb district, Southern Yemen, stating that he wrote it by the desire of Chassan Utz and his sons. The manuscript comprises 133 leaves, with an additional 9 leaves of varied size, measures 25 cm, and has ownership inscriptions and various lists, including prayers, on the endpapers and on additional leaves at the beginning and end.

The source text contains only fragmentary and repetitive bibliographic-style references, including the years 1895, 1894, 1934, 1986, and the notation “25 + 9 kedem [133],” but provides no identifiable object type, material, form, technique, dimensions, or period.

wb_txt_shrt: Manuscript, paper, with original leather binding. Yemen, 19th century. Two Yemenite manuscripts containing prayers and piyyutim, including Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, dream interpretation, tekufot and amulets, blessings and versions of shtarot, Birkat HaMazon with "Chiddushin", eulogies, and a compilation of commentaries. The manuscripts also include prayers and piyyutim of Selichot and Hosha'anot. One scribe's colophon names "Ya'ish son of Yichye Al Tza'adi" and is dated 1864. The bindings are described as ancient leather bindings with leather loops and adornments, worn and wormed, with folk ornamentation. Provenance: Collection of Willy Lindwer.

The first manuscript contains Piyyutim of Bakashot, Tikun Chatzot, interpretation of tekufot, shtarot, “Chiddushin,” Birkat Hamazon with Chiddushin, a eulogy, and a set of comments; it is dated 1864, includes different owner signatures, comprises 131 leaves, measures approximately 16.5 cm, and is in fair condition with bleeding, tears, and stains, with an old leather binding and popular decoration. The verso of the title page bears the note: "Version of blessings and prayers, according to our tradition of the holy congregation throughout Yemenite cities". The title page has an illustrated frame and is inscribed: "Siddur of prayers according to Sephardic tradition, and written for the entire year…". The last approximately 20 leaves, containing prayers for the Festival of Shavuot and Azharot, are in a different handwriting, and some leaves at the end were replaced in another handwriting. The manuscript shows stains, wear, and tears affecting the text of several leaves.

Some leaves were later replaced by another hand; several leaves are missing. The manuscript is in separate leaves, unbound, with spots, holes, tears affecting the text of several leaves, and engravings.

wb_txt_lg: Bronze head of a bull, South Arabian, from the Ḥimyarite Kingdom, dated to the 1st century BC to the 1st century AD. The object is recorded with a height of 8.5 cm.
looted_text: Head, bronze. Thor’s head with two long horns, two clearly defined eyes, ears visible behind the horns, and a rounded nose. Early 1st century C.D.

wb_txt_shrt: Ring, gold, from Near East (Western Asiatic), circa 2nd century AD. Thick D-shaped hoop flaring at the shoulders and widening to encompass the bezel.
wb_txt_lg: Ring, gold, from Ancient South Arabia in the Near East (Western Asiatic), dated circa 2nd century AD. The object has a thick D-shaped hoop that flares outwards at the shoulders before widening to encompass the bezel. The source text also notes a decorated, oval bezel, with incised lines used to create the particles of the hair, tied with a filet. Provenance is given as acquired 1970–1999.
looted_text: Ring, gold. Oval-shaped gold ring in the form of a D, carved into a four-letter support letter, with a carved side figure of a man and a beam below. Dated to the 2nd century AD. Weight 8.74 g.
